advaita.rendering: There is no other cause — none whatsoever — beyond Me, the Paramēśvara (Supreme Lord); I alone am the cause of the world (ahamēva jagatkāraṇam). All beings, this entire universe, is pervaded and threaded through Me, as cloth is strung on its long warp-threads. Śaṅkara immediately presses the hearer: by what dharma (property) is all this strung in You? — inviting the qualification that follows in 7.8–11.
advaita.bhashya_anchor: Śaṅkara: 'matto paramēśvarāt parataraṃ anyat kāraṇāntaraṃ kiñcit nāsti — ahamēva jagatkāraṇam'; 'sarvāṇi bhūtāni … prōtam anustrūtam anuvidhham grathitam — dīrghatantuṣu paṭavat'
advaita.divergence_note: Śaṅkara's sūtra-analogy is cloth-on-warp, not merely bead-on-thread; identity of kāraṇa and kārya is non-dual, not merely hierarchical.

viśiṣṭādvaita.rendering: I surpass all else in two independent respects: as the cause of both prakṛti-s (material and spiritual), and as the śeṣin (sovereign) to whom every cetana (sentient being) stands as śeṣa (subordinate); and beyond these, I excel through My infinite qualities of jñāna, śakti, and bala. All this universe — cit (conscious) and acit (inert), whether in its kārya (effect) state or kāraṇa (causal) state — is My śarīra (body), and so it rests in Me as its ātman, just as beads rest on a thread. The Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad witnesses this: 'yasya pṛthivī śarīram' — earth is the body of that One whose ātman pervades it.
viśiṣṭādvaita.bhashya_anchor: Rāmānuja: 'sarvacetanasyāpi śeṣitayā kāraṇatayā śeṣitayā ca ahaṃ parataraḥ'; 'sarvam idaṃ cidacidvastujātaṃ … maccharīrabhūtaṃ … ātmatayā avasthite mayi prōtam'
viśiṣṭādvaita.divergence_note: Rāmānuja's śarīra-ātman relation preserves real plurality: world and God genuinely differ as body and soul, unlike Śaṅkara's non-dual identity.

dvaita.rendering: I alone am the highest (ahamēva parataraḥ); absolutely nothing other than Me can claim to be supreme. The dependence is total and asymmetric: the jīva (individual soul) is eternally distinct from Hari, radically other, yet wholly reliant — the bead exists in its own right but hangs from the thread, never becoming the thread.
dvaita.bhashya_anchor: Madhva's commentary is brief and pointed: 'ahamēva parataraḥ mattonyat parataraṃ na kiñcidapi' — the exclusive superlative is the entire doctrinal payload.
dvaita.divergence_note: Madhva's bhāṣya on 7.7 is minimal (one sentence); the full Dvaita position is inferred from this and his broader taratamya (hierarchy-of-being) doctrine. Absence of extended commentary here is itself noted.

śuddhādvaita.rendering: One must first know My greatness (māhātmya) — that I am simply the highest, nothing beyond — and from this knowing alone arises proper devotion. All this universe, whatever has form within or is perceived without, is real because it is My svarūpa (essential nature); it is not falsified by avidyā (ignorance) as in Advaita. The world rests in Me as beads on a thread — the ātman-śarīra relation of the Bṛhadāraṇyaka — and this is My own real manifestation (matsvarūpeṇa idaṃ), not a cosmic illusion.
śuddhādvaita.bhashya_anchor: Vallabha: 'matasvarūpeṇa idaṃ na tv avidyākalitam'; cites BṛU 3.7.3 and ŚatPB 14.5.6 as śruti witnesses for ātman-śarīra relation.
śuddhādvaita.divergence_note: Against Śaṅkara, Vallabha insists the world is real as Kṛṣṇa's own form; māhātmya-knowledge (not negation of the world) is the gateway to puṣṭi-bhakti.

bhakti.rendering: Since all this is so, know: beyond Me there is no independently-operating cause — no other lord capable of creation or dissolution (sṛṣṭi-saṃhāra) — and I am likewise the cause of sustenance (sthiti). All this world is threaded in Me (prōtaṃ grathitam āśritam), exactly as the example makes plain. Śrīdhara reads the verse as a smooth logical consequence ('yasmād evaṃ tasmāt') of the prior disclosure of Kṛṣṇa's two natures.
bhakti.bhashya_anchor: Śrīdhara: 'mattaḥ sakāśāt parataraṃ śreṣṭhaṃ jagataḥ sṛṣṭisaṃhārayoḥ svataṃtraṃ kāraṇaṃ kiṃcidapi nāsti'; 'sthitihetur apyahamēvētyāha mayīti'; 'dṛṣṭāntaḥ spaṣṭaḥ'
bhakti.divergence_note: Śrīdhara deliberately glosses the verse lightly ('the example is clear'), letting devotional resonance carry the weight rather than extensive philosophical analysis — a contrast with both Śaṅkara and Madhusūdana.

advaita-bhakti.rendering: From the paramārtha (ultimate) standpoint, nothing separate from Me has any ultimate existence — I am the adhiṣṭhāna (substratum) on which all the apparent forms of the universe are superimposed by māyā, as silver is imagined in a shell, as dream-objects arise in the dreamer's consciousness. From the vyavahāra (conventional) standpoint, all inert multiplicity is strung on Me as its sattā (being) and sphurana (luminosity); the beads on the thread illustrate mere grathitatva (the fact of being threaded), not the full kāraṇatva (causality) — for that, the analogy of golden ornament in gold would be apt. Both readings are simultaneously valid at their respective levels of reality.
advaita-bhakti.bhashya_anchor: Madhusūdana: 'paramārthataḥ … māttor na bhidyata iti' (nothing ultimately differs from Me); 'vyavahāradṛṣṭyā tu mayi sadrūpe … sarvamidaṃ jaḍajātaṃ prōtaṃ grathitaṃ matsattayā sadiva mats-phuranena ca sphuradiva'; 'sūtre maṇigaṇā iva iti … grathitatvamātre dṛṣṭāntaḥ natu kāraṇatve — kanakakundalādivaditi tu yogyo dṛṣṭāntaḥ'
advaita-bhakti.divergence_note: Madhusūdana uniquely distinguishes what the bead-thread image proves (mere threading) from what it does not prove (causality), and supplies the gold-ornament analogy as the complete image — a refinement absent in Śaṅkara and Śrīdhara.

so_what_questions: If nothing exists higher than Kṛṣṇa and the world is strung on Him as beads on a thread, does the thread perish when the beads break — i.e., does the destruction of the world threaten the reality of Brahman at all?

Śaṅkara reads 'prōtam' as non-dual identity (cloth in warp); Rāmānuja reads it as śarīra-ātman (body-soul) relation; Vallabha reads it as real manifestation of Kṛṣṇa's own svarūpa — do all three positions actually contradict each other, or do they disagree only about which level of reality is primary?
Madhusūdana notes the thread-bead image covers only grathitatva (being-threaded), not kāraṇatva (causality) — what does it mean to be ontologically dependent without being causally derived?

The verse addresses Dhanañjaya (Arjuna) by his victory-epithet at the moment of cosmological disclosure. Why does Kṛṣṇa invoke the warrior's name here, and what does that framing say about who is fit to receive this teaching?
If all this is strung in Kṛṣṇa — acit and cit alike, per Rāmānuja — what becomes of the Madhva principle of taratamya (graded difference)? Can genuine hierarchy survive within a single thread?

Madhva's bhāṣya on 7.7 is a single sentence. Is brevity itself a doctrinal statement — asserting that supremacy needs no elaboration — or is it a textual gap requiring supplementation from his other works?

The sūtra-maṇi image appears in the Muṇḍaka and Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣads. By choosing this image, is Kṛṣṇa positioning the Gītā as śruti-equivalent, or is He citing an established śruti metaphor to authorize His own claim?

advaita.application: When confronted with multiplicity — conflicting obligations, competing identities, fractured attention — the practitioner recalls that each thread of concern is already woven into a single ground (adhiṣṭhāna). Rather than resolving conflicts by choosing one strand over another, the practice is to locate the warp: 'What is the consciousness in which all these arise?' This is not passivity but the recognition that no competing object has independent kāraṇatva; each claim on attention borrows reality from the same source.

viśiṣṭādvaita.application: Every person, creature, and inert object encountered today is a śarīra (body) of Bhagavān; none is spiritually inert. The practical consequence: service offered to any being — a colleague, a stranger, a failing student — is offered to the ātman within that body, which is Nārāyaṇa. Kainkarya (loving service) is not confined to the temple; the thread runs through the office, the kitchen, the road.

dvaita.application: The Dvaita practitioner acts from explicit acknowledgment of finitude: 'I am a bead, not the thread; my capacities, my decisions, my virtues are all derived.' This is not self-deprecation but precision — it removes the error of self-sufficiency. Practical discipline: before any significant decision, pause to mark the dependence ('this is Hari's arrangement, not my achievement'), which dissolves the root of ahaṃkāra (ego-attribution) without dissolving the bead's real, distinct existence.

śuddhādvaita.application: Vallabha's reading that the world is Kṛṣṇa's real svarūpa (not illusion) transforms aesthetic experience into sādhana (practice): the beauty of a rāga heard at dawn, a child's laughter, the texture of good work done — these are not distractions from Kṛṣṇa but disclosures of Him. Puṣṭi-mārga practitioners cultivate this recognition as the primary form of worship: receiving the world as prasāda (gracious gift), not as obstacle.

bhakti.application: Śrīdhara's emphasis on sṛṣṭi-saṃhāra-sthiti (creation, dissolution, sustenance) as unified in one cause suggests a practical orientation toward impermanence: beginnings and endings are not opposed events but the same hand moving. When a project ends, a relationship changes, a phase of life closes — the practitioner recognizes this as the one Lord's hand in both the threading and the unthreading. Śrīdhara's 'dṛṣṭāntaḥ spaṣṭaḥ' (the example is clear) counsels against over-intellectualizing: rest in the image itself.

advaita-bhakti.application: Madhusūdana's two-level reading (paramārtha and vyavahāra coexisting) maps onto a practice of holding both scales simultaneously: act in the world with full competence and care (vyavahāra — the beads are real, the thread is real, the stringing matters), while the deeper register holds lightly ('none of this is ultimately other than the ground'). The synthesis prevents both quietist withdrawal (collapsing to paramārtha alone) and anxious grasping (collapsing to vyavahāra alone). Each action is done with full presence and released without residue.

self_assessment: Six bhāṣya-anchored renderings with genuine doctrinal divergence; Madhva bhāṣya sparseness flagged honestly; Madhusūdana's grathitatva vs kāraṇatva distinction and gold-ornament refinement preserved; all Sanskrit terms in Sanskrit form with parenthetical gloss; Śrīdhara content drawn from Sanskrit prose only.
